title: Level 747

='''
Inside the Endless Descent.
'''=
{{Borderimage|image=Abunai Phase.webp|align=center|width=300px|caption=...|id=1}}
{{Collapse|num=1|initial=''Silence is the first thing that settles in, a kind that feels heavy and deliberate, pressing in from every direction.'' Airplanes always have their background noise: the low hum of engines, the whisper of vents, the shuffle of people settling in. But on Flight 747, the silence doesn’t feel like an absence—it feels like a presence. It creeps through the cabin, weaving between the rows and curling up in the corners, making every movement seem too loud. The engines are still there, but their sound is faded, like it’s coming from another world, leaving you with the uneasy sense that the plane itself might be drifting far away from where you are. No one can recall getting on the plane.
Almost everyone who finds themselves here remembers the same scattered flashes: standing in a terminal, making their way down a jet bridge, catching a glimpse of a boarding pass that’s gone blank in their mind. After that, nothing—just the sudden realization they’re already in their seat. The destination sign up front scrolls through ghostly, faded airport names, none of which ring a bell. Occasionally, it lands on a single phrase: "ON TIME." The plane looks like an old Boeing 747, but the inside doesn’t match anything from real airlines. Up front, everything is strangely well-kept: carpets are spotless, lights glow softly, and the leather seats don’t seem worn at all. The coffee makers run as if nothing’s changed, pumping out fresh coffee even though there’s no water to be found anywhere. Open a compartment, and you’ll find blankets neatly folded, safety cards, untouched airline meals, and sometimes even [[Object 1|Almond Water]] in those little plastic cups. It’s always comfortably cool, and the clocks are all stuck on 7:47. Passengers occupy many of these pristine cabins.|final=''Silence is the first thing that settles in, a kind that feels heavy and deliberate, pressing in from every direction.'' Airplanes always have their background noise: the low hum of engines, the whisper of vents, the shuffle of people settling in. But on Flight 747, the silence doesn’t feel like an absence—it feels like a presence. It creeps through the cabin, weaving between the rows and curling up in the corners, making every movement seem too loud. The engines are still there, but their sound is faded, like it’s coming from another world, leaving you with the uneasy sense that the plane itself might be drifting far away from where you are. No one can recall getting on the plane.
Almost everyone who finds themselves here remembers the same scattered flashes: standing in a terminal, making their way down a jet bridge, catching a glimpse of a boarding pass that’s gone blank in their mind. After that, nothing—just the sudden realization they’re already in their seat. The destination sign up front scrolls through ghostly, faded airport names, none of which ring a bell. Occasionally, it lands on a single phrase: "ON TIME." The plane looks like an old Boeing 747, but the inside doesn’t match anything from real airlines. Up front, everything is strangely well-kept: carpets are spotless, lights glow softly, and the leather seats don’t seem worn at all. The coffee makers run as if nothing’s changed, pumping out fresh coffee even though there’s no water to be found anywhere. Open a compartment, and you’ll find blankets neatly folded, safety cards, untouched airline meals, and sometimes even [[Object 1|Almond Water]] in those little plastic cups. It’s always comfortably cool, and the clocks are all stuck on 7:47. Passengers occupy many of these pristine cabins.|body={{Collapse|num=2|initial=
At first, they seem just like any other passengers. You’ll spot a few quietly pretending to read blank newspapers. Some gaze out the windows, but all you’ll see is a shapeless stretch of dull, gray clouds. The flight attendants keep up appearances, gliding past with artificial smiles and trays of food that taste warm and familiar but never really fill you up. Try talking to anyone, and the conversation wanders—normal at first, then drifting into strange, broken talk about missed flights, endless delays, or someone waiting at the arrivals gate. Once you stop trying, it’s like you’re invisible to them. ''But everything shifts the moment you step past Door 3L.'' The change hits so hard, you might think you’ve crossed into a different plane entirely. Carpet squishes underfoot, heavy with years of standing water. Above, ceiling panels dangle, barely hanging on, with thick knots of wire snaking into shadows that go on forever. Most of the seats have given up—just rotting, rusty skeletons now. Mold crawls over the walls in wide, black patches, almost like you’re looking down on a city from the sky. The windows? They fog up and rot away, until there’s nothing left but shredded holes leading out into a darkness where the wings should be, but aren’t. Even with everything falling apart, the plane never actually goes down. The place creaks and shudders with noises no real plane should ever make. Somewhere, something metal groans, like the whole fuselage is being twisted by invisible hands. Overhead bins fly open and slam shut, sometimes for no reason. Oxygen masks sometimes drop and sway, even though the air is completely still. Every so often, a garbled voice comes over the speakers:
''"Ladies and gentlemen, we are beginning our final descent."''
But no descent ever comes.|final=

Some gaze out the windows, but all you’ll see is a shapeless stretch of dull, gray clouds. The flight attendants keep up appearances, gliding past with artificial smiles and trays of food that taste warm and familiar but never really fill you up. Try talking to anyone, and the conversation wanders—normal at first, then drifting into strange, broken talk about missed flights, endless delays, or someone waiting at the arrivals gate. Once you stop trying, it’s like you’re invisible to them. ''But everything shifts the moment you step past Door 3L.'' The change hits so hard, you might think you’ve crossed into a different plane entirely. Carpet squishes underfoot, heavy with years of standing water. Above, ceiling panels dangle, barely hanging on, with thick knots of wire snaking into shadows that go on forever. Most of the seats have given up—just rotting, rusty skeletons now. Mold crawls over the walls in wide, black patches, almost like you’re looking down on a city from the sky. The windows? They fog up and rot away, until there’s nothing left but shredded holes leading out into a darkness where the wings should be, but aren’t. Even with everything falling apart, the plane never actually goes down. The place creaks and shudders with noises no real plane should ever make. Somewhere, something metal groans, like the whole fuselage is being twisted by invisible hands. Overhead bins fly open and slam shut, sometimes for no reason. Oxygen masks sometimes drop and sway, even though the air is completely still. Every so often, a garbled voice comes over the speakers:
''"Ladies and gentlemen, we are beginning our final descent."''
But no descent ever comes.|body=
Some hallways twist into shapes that shouldn’t exist. One minute you’re shuffling through cramped economy, and the next, you’re standing in a first-class lounge you swear you passed hours ago. The bathrooms sometimes hide endless tunnels filled with flickering lights and echoing drips—passages that go on much farther than any plane should allow. Here and there, a cargo door might creak open to reveal a deserted terminal or a yawning hangar, each one leading off into stranger places you’d rather not see. ''But it’s the back of the plane that truly changes the environment.'' Back here, the last twenty rows flicker in and out of decay. Sometimes it looks spotless—like the crew just finished cleaning for a new flight. Blink, and suddenly everything’s rusted, the seats collapsed in on themselves, and the floor gives way to the plane’s bare bones. Whenever things shift, you’ll hear footsteps, muffled and far off, coming from behind the last wall. ''Nobody’s ever found a way past that wall. Still, there’s always something moving behind it, coming closer.'' No one’s ever caught a glimpse of what’s back there. If you linger, you might hear the squeak of suitcase wheels or someone clearing their throat just on the other side. Anyone who gets too curious usually vanishes before anyone else can get close. ''The crew doesn’t mention the missing. Not ever. They just keep making their rounds, trays in hand, like nothing’s wrong.'' Strangest of all, the plane sometimes fixes itself. Torn seats are suddenly patched, rust vanishes like it was never there, and broken lights blink back to life—at least for a while. Sooner or later, everything falls apart again. It’s like the whole place is stuck in a loop, trapped between falling apart and being brand new. Old hands tend to stay put on this flight. Compared to most places out there, Flight 747 is almost safe. There’s clean water up front, wrapped meals that don’t taste half bad, and not much trouble from anything dangerous. Some folks have even made little camps in the cabins—stashes of supplies, makeshift bunks, the works. The attendants usually let everyone be, so long as no one messes with the controls or tries to barge into the cockpit. Nobody’s ever gotten the cockpit door to budge. Knock all you want—there’s never an answer. If you press your ear against the door, you’ll sometimes catch two voices quietly chatting about the weather over an ocean nobody’s seen in ages. No one’s managed to record the voices, though. Some say '''Flight 747''' just wanders, never landing, slipping between lost corners of the Backrooms with a cabin full of people who never arrive. Others think it’s not moving at all—just frozen in time, stuck between landing and crashing for as long as anyone can remember. Whatever’s really going on, every so often the seatbelt sign lights up with a soft ding. The captain’s calm voice drifts out over the speakers. ''"Ladies and gentlemen, we’ve hit a bit of turbulence. Please stay in your seats. We’ll be there soon."'' ''But nobody’s ever made it to the end.''
='''
Flight 747: "The Endless Flight"
'''=
{{Image Banner|image=Airplanewing.jpg|caption=
"We are now cruising at 11,000 meters."
"Feel free to unbuckle your seatbelt and enjoy the ride. We will be arriving in approximately Error = null hours."
}}
{{Class Custom Image|hexagon=2|bg-image=Abunai Phase.webp|header=Survival Difficulty: Class|class=2|safety=Safe|security=Secure|entity=Moderate Entity Presence|text-color=white}}
You know how planes are often crowded? Usually, almost every seat is taken up. So it must be a bit nerve-racking to see this level. Not a single soul in sight. '''Level 747''' is the '''748th''' level of '''[[The Backrooms]]'''. ='''
Description
'''= ----
{{Borderimage|image=Taiyo Phase.webp|align=right|width=300px|caption=Photo captured by wanderer showcasing the aircraft, captured on July 8th, 2009.|id=747}} '''Level 747''' feels like stepping into a never-ending '''Boeing 747-400''', flying through empty skies. From the outside, the plane looks ordinary, but anyone who explores the inside finds a maze of corridors, cabins, service areas, and storage rooms stretching for kilometers. The space seems impossible, far larger than any real aircraft. Some parts are spotless and livable, while others are falling apart and left to rot. The line between these areas is never fixed and can shift without warning. Most of the front of the plane and some of the upper deck are surprisingly livable. These spaces look just like the inside of a real passenger jet. The lights give off a steady, welcoming glow, and the air feels fresh from working vents. Bathrooms have clean water, and the kitchens keep restocking with packaged meals, bottles of water, and sometimes '''[[Object 1|Almond Water]]'''. The seats are all in good shape, with blankets and emergency gear stored overhead. Even though nobody can find a power source, the emergency lights always work. The temperature stays pleasant, and it’s quiet enough for people to settle in for a while. Small groups have set up camps in these comfortable areas. {{Borderimage|image=Boarding hall.webp|align=right|width=300px|caption=The spawning point on '''Level 747''', before one enters the plane.|id=748}} The abandoned sections feel worlds apart from the livable cabins up front. Most of the carpet is ripped up or gone, leaving only rusted beams underfoot. Ceiling tiles dangle at odd angles, and tangled wires disappear into the darkness above. Rows of seats have fallen apart, rust eating away at the frames, and many windows are so badly cracked you can’t see a thing, while others have been replaced by gaping holes that look out into a never-ending black sky. Dampness seeps through every surface, even though there’s no clear source for the moisture. Mold and rust climb the walls and fill the overhead bins. The whole place is filled with the sound of metal straining and groaning, but somehow, the structure always holds. {{Borderimage|image=Abunai Phase.webp|align=right|width=300px|caption=Photo captured by wanderer showcasing the destroyed phase. Captured on November 12th, 2015.|id=749}} The plane ignores the usual rules of space. You might step through one cabin, only to find yourself reentering another part of the aircraft without warning. Service corridors can stretch on for kilometers, never leading to an exit. Sometimes, cargo holds open into vast storerooms filled with forgotten luggage, old tools, and shipping containers stamped with logos no one recognizes. Certain maintenance shafts drop into areas unrelated to airplanes, suggesting that this level intersects with other parts of the Backrooms. Even though it always seems to be flying, no one has ever seen the wings move or felt any turbulence. The plane never changes altitude, at least not in any way you can sense. No one has ever managed to reach the cockpit. The door is reinforced and unbreakable, refusing to budge no matter what anyone tries. Still, you might catch the sound of pilots talking quietly on the other side, often mentioning places or weather that don’t match anything in the real world. Every so often, the intercom crackles to life with flight updates, arrival times, or warnings about an imminent descent. These announcements never line up with anything actually happening on the plane. Most of the people you see here look just like regular passengers and crew you’d find on any commercial flight. They spend their time in quiet loops—reading, serving food, adjusting bags, or just watching the clouds drift by. If you try to talk to them, they might respond for a moment, but the conversation soon trails off into confusion or silence. They’re never aggressive, and they keep going about their business no matter what happens around them. While most of '''Level 747''' feels safe compared to other transit levels, heading toward the back is a different story. The further you go, the darker and more confusing things become. Hallways twist, floors become uneven, and it’s easy to lose your way. People who vanish in these parts almost never come back. Nobody really knows why, but some say they hear footsteps, suitcase wheels, or voices in the distance just before someone goes missing. {{Borderimage|image=Firstclass.png|align=right|width=300px|caption=An ordinary plane, with some [[Entity 9|Facelings]] far away.|id=750}} Looking out any window, you’ll find nothing but endless grey clouds, blackness, or a blank sky. It doesn’t matter which way the plane seems to fly—there’s never a sign of land or anything familiar. No one has ever managed to open a window or force an emergency exit, no matter how hard they try. As far as anyone knows, the inside of the plane is all there is to this level. Even with its strange atmosphere, '''Level 747''' is considered a good place to rest. There’s always food, water, warmth, and safe spots to sleep. Still, those who know the place well warn against spending too much time exploring the back of the plane. The further you go, the less stable things get, and the more often people seem to vanish without a trace. ='''
Entities
'''= ---- Flight Attendants
Threat Level: 0 ---- '''Flight Attendants''' look and act almost exactly like real crew members, right down to their spotless uniforms that seem to come from every era between the 1960s and early 2000s. They move through the cabin without pause, serving food, folding blankets, tidying up the overhead bins, and even going through the motions of safety demonstrations—though the plane never actually takes off. They’re always polite, but their answers are short, and they never drop their pleasant smiles. If you’re courteous, they might hand out snacks, bottles of water, Almond Water, or whatever supplies you need. Just don’t make a mess or get in their way—if you do, every Flight Attendant nearby will simply stop what they’re doing and watch you in silence until you go somewhere else. ---- Passengers
Threat Level: 0 ---- Passengers fill row after row of seats, scattered all across the plane. Most spend their time flipping through empty magazines, dozing off, or gazing at the clouds outside. They almost never start a conversation, and many won’t even look up if you try talking to them. A Passenger may occasionally ask simple questions such as: *"Is this your stop?" *"What time are we landing?" *"Have you seen my suitcase?" If you reply calmly and straightforwardly, the Passenger usually drops the subject and goes back to what they were doing. But if your answer doesn’t make sense, or you contradict them, it’s as if you start to fade from their memory. Soon, they just return to their routine as if you were never there. ---- Gatekeepers
Threat Level: 2 ---- '''Gatekeepers''' are found lurking in the deserted parts of the plane, close to emergency exits and maintenance doors. They have the look of airport security, dressed in worn uniforms with their faces always hidden by their caps. They keep their distance, watching anyone who passes by without ever saying a word. As long as you leave the plane alone, they stay out of your way. But if you try to break open an exit, mess with the plane, or go near the cockpit, they quickly turn aggressive. If they decide to chase someone, they cover ground at an unsettling speed, slipping through the narrow aisles even though it looks like they’re just walking. ---- Baggage Crawlers
Threat Level: 3 ---- A wanderer will find these creatures tucked away in cargo holds and luggage storage compartments. '''Baggage Crawlers''' look like huge heaps of old suitcases, backpacks, tangled straps, and scraps of clothing. They move around on a tangle of thin black limbs that stay hidden under the mess. Most of the time, these things stay perfectly still unless someone messes with the luggage nearby. If you wake one up, it will haul itself across the floor faster than you’d think, making a noise that sounds just like suitcase wheels rolling over tile. Luckily, if one leaves the cargo area, they quickly lose interest and settle back down. ---- The Last Passenger
Threat Level: 4 ---- There’s never more than one '''Last Passenger''' on the plane at any moment. One will spot this figure in the last few rows of the plane, always in the sections where everything is falling apart. From far away, it just looks like another traveler resting in silence. As one gets closer, something starts to feel off. The boarding pass it holds is completely blank. Its clothing seems to rot away over time. No matter how hard a wanderer looks, one can never quite see its face. If the '''Last Passenger''' ever rises from its seat, all the lights nearby go out at once. People who have seen this say they hear footsteps coming up behind them, no matter where the figure actually is. It’s safest to leave the area right away and not wait for the lights to come back on. ='''
Colonies & Outposts
'''= ---- Due to the level's vast size, no major outposts have been installed; however, a few small groups have been formed: ===The Flight Attendants=== *Provides food and assistance *Open to trade *Accepts all wanderers *Nomadic group of 26 people (19 wanderers and 7 '''[[Entity 9|False Facelings]]''') ===Cargo Crew=== *Loots overhead bins and cargo areas *Open to trade *Doesn't accept all wanderers but will allow volunteers to help *Nomadic group of 14 people (13 wanderers and 1 '''[[Level 107|Cascadan]]''') ='''
Entrances & Exits
'''= ---- ===Entrances=== There are several documented methods of entering '''Level 747''', though none are completely reliable. *Most people enter through empty airport terminals like '''[[Level 36]]'''. Sometimes, a boarding gate that looks abandoned will suddenly be open for use. If one steps onto a jet bridge leading to a parked '''Boeing 747''', one might find themselves inside '''Level 747''' instead of a regular plane. *Some other levels with crashed or deserted planes are rumored to link up with '''Level 747'''. If you go into the intact fuselage of a '''Boeing 747''' and stay inside for about ten minutes, the area around you can slowly turn into the well-kept cabins of this level. *Some industrial or maintenance levels like '''[[Level 877]]''' have service tunnels with airline maintenance signs posted along the walls. If you keep going down these hallways, you might eventually find a service hatch that opens right into one of '''Level 747’s''' engineering rooms. *Some people say they hear airport announcements even when there’s no reason for them. If you follow the directions from these disembodied voices, you might end up at a normal-looking boarding gate. Step through, and you’re instantly on '''Level 747''', already sitting in a passenger seat, with no recollection of how you got there. *On rare occasions, a working elevator in a transportation-themed level opens straight into one of the plane’s galleys or cargo areas. These kinds of entrances happen without warning and can’t be made to appear on purpose. ===Exits=== Leaving '''Level 747''' is considerably more difficult than entering, although several exits have been documented. *Certain cargo containers found on the lower cargo deck occasionally contain functioning doors instead of luggage. Passing through one may lead to Backrooms levels themed around warehouses, storage, or industry. These exits are considered the most reliable. *Sometimes, if you find a maintenance shaft behind a wall panel, it leads straight into a utility tunnel from another level. Where these tunnels end up changes every time, and more often than not, the passage collapses or vanishes once it’s been used. *On the rarest occasions, an emergency exit will actually open and send out an evacuation slide. If you slide down, you won’t end up outside the plane but will find yourself in a different transportation-themed part of the Backrooms. Each time, the destination seems to be different. *In the ruined back of the plane, there’s a sealed bulkhead where people often hear inexplicable footsteps. Once in a great while, someone finds the door just barely open. If you go in, you might come out in a random Backrooms level—sometimes hours or even days later. Because so many never return, this way out is best avoided. *Every once in a long while, after the captain’s voice comes over the speaker and says, "Welcome to your destination," the closest cabin door will work like a real exit. If you step through, you’ll end up in a random habitable level instead of an airport. This almost never happens, and no one’s figured out how to make it happen on purpose.
}} }}
